related_chapters.subject: Grace Hall Miller
related_chapters.why: Smith documents Southern courts that returned no indictment when a white man killed a Black man, and Miller recounts the white neighbor who shot her husband Hosie in the back and was never convicted across three trials.

related_chapters.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
related_chapters.chapter_title: Walking in the Front Door of the Courthouse
related_chapters.topic: A Young Black Attorney in the 1950s
related_chapters.why: Smith notes that only here and there a Black lawyer is permitted to appear in Southern courts, and Finney describes practicing as a young Black attorney in the 1950s before becoming the first Black Chief Justice of South Carolina's Supreme Court since Reconstruction.

related_chapters.subject: Cecil J. Williams
related_chapters.why: Smith catalogs the Jim Crow statutes that nullified the war amendments, and Williams, who photographed the Orangeburg movement, speaks to the regime of segregation written into law.

related_chapters.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
related_chapters.why: Smith places the Black citizen's hope in constitutional litigation to restore equal protection, and Suyat Marshall describes her work at the NAACP national office on Brown v. Board of Education, which overturned the separate-but-equal doctrine Smith protested.

related_chapters.subject: Clarence Magee
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Cat-and-Mouse Test, and the Lawsuit
related_chapters.why: Smith details the registrar tactics built to exclude Black voters through impossible tests, and Magee recounts the cat-and-mouse registration test under registrar Theron Lynd and the lawsuit brought against it in Hattiesburg.

related_chapters.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
related_chapters.why: Smith praises the rare Southern judges who stood for fairness inside the segregated courtroom, and Perry recalls the Jim Crow facilities and Judge Waring's rulings that shaped his decision to study law.

top_voice.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
top_voice.reason: Finney embodies the answer to Smith's plea: the Black attorney Smith said was barely permitted to appear in court rose to become the first Black Chief Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since Reconstruction.

related_chapters.subject: Harold K. Brown
related_chapters.chapter_title: They Couldn't Put Those Two Words Together
related_chapters.why: Fortune argues the race advances through economic self-reliance and the individual building wealth, and Brown frames Black wealth and working together as the unfinished economic step beyond the civil-rights movement.

related_chapters.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
related_chapters.chapter_title: Picked Them Up in the 1870 Census
related_chapters.why: Fortune predicts the Black farmer will become the landowning agriculturist of the South rather than a peon, and Sherrod traces her landowning farm family at the Hawkins Settlement back through the 1870 census.

related_chapters.subject: Robert Brown
related_chapters.part_title: B and C Associates and the Movement
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Power of the Black Dollar
related_chapters.why: Fortune ties the race's standing to Black commercial development, and Brown, who built the firm B and C Associates, speaks to the power of the Black dollar and selling economic power.

related_chapters.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller
related_chapters.part_title: School, Fair Street, and a Near-Lynching
related_chapters.chapter_title: Fair Street: A Thriving Black Main Street
related_chapters.why: Fortune points to the institutions Black communities built as proof of progress, and the Millers describe Fair Street, the thriving Black business district of Port Gibson.

related_chapters.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
related_chapters.part_title: New Communities and the Land Suit
related_chapters.why: Fortune envisions collective Black landholding as the route out of economic dependence, and Young recounts founding New Communities Inc. on a large tract organized for self-sufficiency.

related_chapters.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
related_chapters.part_title: The Color Line in the Blood
related_chapters.chapter_title: I Want to Be Just an American
related_chapters.why: Fortune writes on mixed ancestry and the wish to be received as an indivisible part of the nation rather than a fixed caste, and this chapter on the color line in the blood carries the claim I want to be just an American.

top_voice.subject: Harold K. Brown
top_voice.reason: Brown's argument that economics is the next step and that Black wealth must be built together restates Fortune's central thesis that the race advances through economic self-reliance.

related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.why: Du Bois argues the college-trained few must lead and build the institutions that teach the rest, and Terry recounts building the W. E. B. Du Bois Department of Afro-American Studies from a program into a department.

related_chapters.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
related_chapters.chapter_title: It Does Not Have to Be This Way
related_chapters.why: Du Bois holds that the race is saved by developing its exceptional, highly educated few, and Hrabowski describes realizing the Meyerhoff Scholars Program, which produces Black PhDs in science at scale.

related_chapters.subject: Lonnie C. King
related_chapters.why: Du Bois casts the college-educated as the leaders of thought among their people, and King recounts the Atlanta University Center students having Roslyn Pope write the 1960 Appeal for Human Rights.

related_chapters.subject: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
related_chapters.chapter_title: My Child Has to Learn How to Think
related_chapters.why: Du Bois insists the central task is training teachers, and teachers of teachers, and Glascoe's charge to the young is about teaching teachers to think.

related_chapters.subject: Amos C. Brown
related_chapters.chapter_title: Pass It On and Arriving at Morehouse
related_chapters.why: Du Bois names the Black colleges as the training ground of leaders, and Brown describes arriving at Morehouse, where he later studied under Martin Luther King Jr., and the charge to pass it on.

related_chapters.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton
related_chapters.why: Du Bois frames the educated leader's duty as teaching the mass through trained teachers, and Cotton describes the SCLC Citizenship Education teaching team of Septima Clark, Andrew Young, and herself that trained thousands of grassroots organizers.

top_voice.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
top_voice.reason: Terry moved from a Bennett College sit-in participant to building the W. E. B. Du Bois Department of Afro-American Studies, a direct enactment of Du Bois's call for college-trained leaders to build the institutions that teach the race.
